agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Integration architecture for a logistics platform connecting three external systems (SAP ERP via SFTP/XML, DHL Carrier via REST/webhooks, Analytics via Kafka) using an event-driven backbone with dedicated Anti-Corruption Layers per integration. The design treats internal domain events as the single source of truth, with each external system adapter translating to/from the internal model independently. This prevents any external system's model or protocol from leaking into the core domain, keeps integrations independently deployable and testable, and naturally feeds the Kafka event stream required by Analytics.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Domain Service","responsibility":"Owns the order lifecycle: OrderPlaced → OrderConfirmed. Enforces business invariants (e.g., order must have valid line items before confirmation). Publishes domain events to the internal event bus.","boundaries":"Knows nothing about SAP, DHL, or Kafka. Speaks only in domain events and domain commands. Persists order aggregates."},{"name":"Shipment Domain Service","responsibility":"Owns the shipment lifecycle: ShipmentCreated → DeliveryAttempted → DeliveryCompleted / DeliveryFailed. Tracks shipment state machine transitions and enforces invariants (e.g., cannot complete an already-failed delivery).","boundaries":"Knows nothing about external carrier protocols. Receives commands from the Carrier ACL and publishes domain events."},{"name":"Internal Event Bus","responsibility":"Brokers all domain events within the platform. Provides at-least-once delivery guarantees with ordered partitioning by aggregate ID. Implemented as an internal Kafka topic (or a lightweight broker like NATS if latency is paramount) — distinct from the external Analytics stream.","boundaries":"Carries only canonical domain events. No external system payloads traverse this bus."},{"name":"SAP ACL (Anti-Corruption Layer)","responsibility":"Polls SFTP endpoint every 15 minutes. Parses SAP IDoc/XML format into domain commands (ConfirmOrder). Handles XML schema versioning, character encoding issues, and SAP-specific quirks (e.g., leading-zero material numbers). Writes acknowledgment files back to SFTP on success/failure.","boundaries":"Isolated adapter. Owns the SFTP connection, XML parsing, and SAP-to-domain translation. Failures here do not affect other integrations. Has its own retry queue and dead-letter directory."},{"name":"DHL ACL (Anti-Corruption Layer)","responsibility":"Outbound: translates ShipmentCreated domain events into DHL REST API calls to create shipments and request tracking. Inbound: receives DHL webhook callbacks, validates signatures, translates DHL status codes into domain commands (RecordDeliveryAttempt, CompleteDelivery, FailDelivery). Must respond to webhooks within 500ms SLA.","boundaries":"Owns the HTTP client for DHL API, the webhook endpoint, and the DHL-to-domain status mapping table. Webhook handler is stateless and fast — it enqueues a command and returns 200 immediately, with processing happening asynchronously."},{"name":"Analytics Event Publisher","responsibility":"Subscribes to all domain events on the internal event bus. Translates them into the Analytics platform's published event schema and produces them to the external Kafka topic. Handles schema evolution via a Schema Registry (Avro or Protobuf).","boundaries":"Read-only consumer of internal events. Owns the external Kafka producer connection and the domain-to-analytics schema mapping. Has no write path back into the domain."},{"name":"Integration Monitor","responsibility":"Observes health of all three integration channels. Tracks SFTP poll success/failure rates, DHL API latency and error rates, Kafka producer lag. Exposes metrics (Prometheus) and triggers alerts on SLA breaches.","boundaries":"Observability-only. No business logic. Reads metrics from each ACL's instrumentation."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"SAP ACL","to":"Order Domain Service","contract":"Emits ConfirmOrder command with {orderId, sapDocumentNumber, confirmedLineItems[], confirmedAt}. Idempotent — duplicate SAP confirmations for the same order are safely ignored.","coupling_type":"loose — async command via internal bus"},{"from":"Order Domain Service","to":"Internal Event Bus","contract":"Publishes OrderPlaced{orderId, items[], placedAt} and OrderConfirmed{orderId, confirmedAt, sapRef}","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven, fire-and-forget"},{"from":"Shipment Domain Service","to":"Internal Event Bus","contract":"Publishes ShipmentCreated{shipmentId, orderId, carrier, createdAt}, DeliveryAttempted{shipmentId, attemptNumber, timestamp}, DeliveryCompleted{shipmentId, completedAt, signedBy}, DeliveryFailed{shipmentId, failureReason, failedAt}","coupling_type":"loose — event-driven"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"DHL ACL","contract":"DHL ACL subscribes to ShipmentCreated events. On receipt, calls DHL REST API POST /shipments with translated payload. Correlation: stores {shipmentId ↔ dhlTrackingNumber} mapping.","coupling_type":"loose — event subscription"},{"from":"DHL Webhook Endpoint","to":"DHL ACL","contract":"DHL POSTs status callbacks to /webhooks/dhl with HMAC-signed JSON payload. ACL validates signature, maps DHL status enum to domain command, enqueues command, returns HTTP 200 within 500ms.","coupling_type":"loose — webhook with async processing"},{"from":"DHL ACL","to":"Shipment Domain Service","contract":"Emits RecordDeliveryAttempt, CompleteDelivery, or FailDelivery commands. Idempotent by {dhlTrackingNumber, eventTimestamp}.","coupling_type":"loose — async command"},{"from":"Internal Event Bus","to":"Analytics Event Publisher","contract":"Subscribes to ALL domain events. One-way flow. Events are enriched with metadata (correlationId, eventVersion, publishedAt) and produced to external Kafka topic in Avro format.","coupling_type":"loose — event subscription, read-only"}],"data_flow":"1. Orders enter the system via API → Order Domain Service publishes OrderPlaced to Internal Event Bus. 2. SAP ACL polls SFTP every 15min, parses XML, issues ConfirmOrder command → Order Domain Service validates and publishes OrderConfirmed. 3. Fulfillment logic (out of scope) triggers ShipmentCreated → DHL ACL picks up event, calls DHL REST API, stores tracking number mapping. 4. DHL sends webhook callbacks → DHL ACL webhook endpoint accepts within 500ms, enqueues async processing → translates to domain command → Shipment Domain Service updates state and publishes DeliveryAttempted / DeliveryCompleted / DeliveryFailed. 5. Analytics Event Publisher tails ALL internal events and produces them to external Kafka topic in real-time. 6. Every step is idempotent and independently retriable.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Anti-Corruption Layer (per integration)","rationale":"Each external system has a radically different protocol (SFTP/XML, REST/webhook, Kafka), data model, and failure mode. ACLs prevent any external model from contaminating the core domain. They also make each integration independently replaceable — swapping DHL for FedEx only changes one ACL."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (internal event bus)","rationale":"Decouples the domain services from each other and from all integrations. Enables the Analytics requirement naturally — the event stream already exists. Supports temporal decoupling so that slow consumers (SAP ACL) don't block fast ones (DHL webhook)."},{"pattern":"Async Command via Webhook + Queue","rationale":"DHL's 500ms SLA on webhook response mandates that the webhook handler do minimal work — validate, enqueue, return. Actual domain processing happens asynchronously from the queue. This separates the protocol constraint from the business logic constraint."},{"pattern":"Polling Consumer (SAP SFTP)","rationale":"SAP's SFTP/XML integration is inherently batch-oriented. A scheduled polling consumer with idempotent processing (tracking processed file names) fits this constraint naturally without trying to force SAP into an event-driven model."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Ensures domain state changes and event publication are atomic. When Order Domain Service confirms an order, it writes the OrderConfirmed event to an outbox table in the same transaction. A relay process publishes outbox entries to the event bus. This prevents ghost events (event published but state not saved) or lost events (state saved but event lost)."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Receivers","rationale":"Every command and event handler is idempotent. SAP may send duplicate files, DHL may retry webhooks, Kafka may redeliver. Idempotency keys (orderId+sapDocRef, trackingNumber+timestamp, eventId) ensure exactly-once semantics at the business level despite at-least-once delivery."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: Placed → Confirmed → Fulfilled, invariants: all line items must be valid, confirmation requires SAP reference)"]},{"name":"Shipment & Delivery","classification":"core","aggregates":["Shipment (root: shipmentId, states: Created → InTransit → Delivered | Failed, invariants: max 3 delivery attempts before escalation, cannot transition from terminal states)"]},{"name":"ERP Integration (SAP)","classification":"generic","aggregates":["SFTPPollState (tracks last-processed file marker, poll schedule, retry state)","InboundFileLog (idempotency registry for processed XML files)"]},{"name":"Carrier Integration (DHL)","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CarrierMapping (tracks shipmentId ↔ dhlTrackingNumber, webhook event dedup)"]},{"name":"Analytics Publishing","classification":"generic","aggregates":["PublisherOffset (tracks last-published event offset per partition for exactly-once delivery to external Kafka)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"ERP Integration (SAP)","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Order Management defines the canonical OrderConfirmed model. SAP ACL conforms to it. SAP ACL is the customer requesting confirmations; Order Management is the supplier that dictates the domain model."},{"upstream":"Shipment & Delivery","downstream":"Carrier Integration (DHL)","relationship":"Customer-Supplier with ACL — Shipment context defines ShipmentCreated and delivery events. DHL ACL translates between DHL's model and the domain model. ACL protects the domain from DHL-specific status codes and payload structures."},{"upstream":"Order Management + Shipment & Delivery","downstream":"Analytics Publishing","relationship":"Conformist — Analytics Publisher conforms to whatever events the upstream contexts publish. It has no influence on the domain model; it simply translates and forwards."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Internal event bus as backbone (rather than direct service-to-service calls)","gains":"Temporal decoupling, natural fit for Analytics Kafka requirement, independent scalability of consumers, easy to add new integrations by subscribing to existing events","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency between services, harder to debug end-to-end flows without correlation IDs and distributed tracing, additional infrastructure (broker) to operate","alternatives_considered":["Direct synchronous REST calls between services — simpler to trace but creates tight coupling and cascade failures","Shared database with polling — simpler infrastructure but creates hidden coupling via schema and contention"]},{"decision":"Separate internal event bus from external Analytics Kafka topic","gains":"Internal events can evolve independently of the Analytics contract. Internal bus can use a simpler/faster broker if Kafka is overkill internally. Analytics schema is explicitly versioned and governed.","sacrifices":"Additional translation layer and potential latency between internal event and Analytics publication (typically < 100ms)","alternatives_considered":["Single Kafka cluster for both internal and external — simpler but couples internal event schema evolution to external consumers, any schema change breaks Analytics"]},{"decision":"Webhook handler returns 200 immediately, processes asynchronously","gains":"Meets 500ms SLA reliably regardless of domain processing time. Domain logic can take seconds without violating carrier contract.","sacrifices":"DHL sees success even if downstream processing fails. Must handle failed processing via retries and dead-letter queues. More complex error handling.","alternatives_considered":["Synchronous processing in webhook handler — simpler but risks SLA breach under load or when domain service is slow","Return 202 Accepted with callback — DHL may not support this pattern"]},{"decision":"One ACL per external system (not a shared integration layer)","gains":"Each integration evolves independently. SAP team can change XML parsing without affecting DHL webhook handling. Failure isolation — SAP SFTP outage doesn't impact DHL processing.","sacrifices":"Some code duplication across ACLs (retry logic, monitoring instrumentation). More deployable units to manage.","alternatives_considered":["Shared ESB/integration platform — centralizes integration logic but becomes a single point of failure and a coupling magnet"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Carrier Integration","evolution_path":"Replace DHL with FedEx, UPS, or multi-carrier by implementing a new ACL that subscribes to the same ShipmentCreated events. Can run multiple carrier ACLs simultaneously for A/B testing or gradual migration. Core domain unchanged."},{"area":"ERP Integration","evolution_path":"Migrate from SFTP/XML to SAP S/4HANA REST API by replacing the SAP ACL internals. The domain command interface (ConfirmOrder) stays stable. Can run old and new ACLs in parallel during migration."},{"area":"Analytics Platform","evolution_path":"Switch from Kafka to a different streaming platform (Kinesis, Pulsar) by replacing the Analytics Event Publisher. Internal event bus and domain are unaffected."},{"area":"New Integrations","evolution_path":"Adding a new external system (e.g., warehouse management, customer notification service) requires only a new subscriber to the internal event bus. Zero changes to existing components."},{"area":"Event Schema Evolution","evolution_path":"Internal events use explicit version numbers. Consumers handle multiple versions during transition periods. Schema Registry enforces backward compatibility on the Analytics Kafka topic."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Domain Event Schema (internal)","reason":"All consumers depend on the shape of domain events. Changing an event's structure requires coordinated consumer updates. This rigidity is intentional — the domain event schema IS the system's contract. Use additive-only changes and version fields to manage evolution."},{"area":"Aggregate Invariants","reason":"Order and Shipment state machines enforce strict business rules (e.g., no delivery after failure without explicit re-attempt). These must remain rigid to prevent data corruption. Changes require careful domain analysis."},{"area":"Idempotency Keys","reason":"The choice of idempotency key composition (e.g., orderId+sapDocRef) is baked into each ACL and domain service. Changing these after production data exists requires careful migration."},{"area":"500ms Webhook SLA","reason":"The DHL webhook response time constraint drives the async-enqueue architecture. This constraint propagates into infrastructure sizing and monitoring thresholds. If the SLA changes, the architecture supports it — but the monitoring and alerting configuration must be updated."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Event ordering: if OrderConfirmed arrives at Analytics before OrderPlaced due to race conditions or parallel processing","severity":"high","mitigation":"Partition internal event bus by orderId to guarantee per-order ordering. Use monotonic sequence numbers within each aggregate. Analytics Publisher preserves partition assignment."},{"risk":"SAP SFTP polling misses files or double-processes them during clock skew or directory listing inconsistencies","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Track processed files by name+checksum in InboundFileLog. Use move-after-process pattern: poll from /incoming, move to /processed after successful handling, move to /failed on error. Idempotent ConfirmOrder command provides safety net."},{"risk":"DHL webhook replay or out-of-order delivery (e.g., DeliveryCompleted webhook arrives before ShipmentCreated event is processed)","severity":"high","mitigation":"DHL ACL buffers webhook events that reference unknown shipments in a retry queue with exponential backoff. CarrierMapping lookup failure triggers re-enqueue, not rejection. Dedup by {trackingNumber, dhlEventId} prevents duplicate processing."},{"risk":"Internal event bus becomes single point of failure — if it goes down, all integrations stall","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Deploy event bus (Kafka) as a multi-broker, multi-AZ cluster with replication factor ≥ 3. Each ACL has a local retry/buffer mechanism (transactional outbox for producers, consumer offset tracking for consumers). System degrades gracefully: SAP ACL queues files locally, DHL ACL buffers webhook payloads in-memory/on-disk."},{"risk":"Schema drift between internal domain events and Analytics Kafka topic due to missed translation updates","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Schema Registry with compatibility checks on the Analytics topic. CI pipeline validates that Analytics Publisher handles all current event versions. Contract tests between domain services and Analytics Publisher."},{"risk":"Transactional outbox relay introduces latency or fails silently, causing events to be delayed or lost","severity":"high","mitigation":"Monitor outbox table size as a key metric — growing backlog indicates relay failure. Relay process has its own health check and alerting. Outbox entries have created_at timestamps; alert if any entry is older than 5 seconds."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement in three phases. Phase 1: Core domain services (Order, Shipment) with internal event bus and transactional outbox. Validate event flow with integration tests. Phase 2: SAP ACL and DHL ACL in parallel — these are independent and can be built by separate teams. Phase 3: Analytics Event Publisher — this is the simplest integration since it's read-only. Each phase is independently deployable and testable.","files_to_create_or_modify":["src/domain/order/OrderAggregate.{ts|java} — Order state machine and invariants","src/domain/order/events/ — OrderPlaced, OrderConfirmed event definitions","src/domain/shipment/ShipmentAggregate.{ts|java} — Shipment state machine","src/domain/shipment/events/ — ShipmentCreated, DeliveryAttempted, DeliveryCompleted, DeliveryFailed","src/infrastructure/eventbus/TransactionalOutbox.{ts|java} — Outbox table + relay","src/infrastructure/eventbus/EventBusProducer.{ts|java} — Internal event publisher","src/integration/sap/SapAcl.{ts|java} — SFTP poller, XML parser, ConfirmOrder command emitter","src/integration/sap/SapXmlParser.{ts|java} — SAP IDoc XML to domain model translation","src/integration/sap/InboundFileLog.{ts|java} — Processed file tracking for idempotency","src/integration/dhl/DhlAcl.{ts|java} — Outbound API client + inbound webhook handler","src/integration/dhl/DhlWebhookController.{ts|java} — HTTP endpoint, signature validation, async enqueue","src/integration/dhl/DhlStatusMapper.{ts|java} — DHL status code to domain command mapping","src/integration/dhl/CarrierMappingRepository.{ts|java} — shipmentId ↔ trackingNumber store","src/integration/analytics/AnalyticsEventPublisher.{ts|java} — Internal event subscriber + Kafka producer","src/integration/analytics/AnalyticsSchemaMapper.{ts|java} — Domain event to Avro schema translation","src/monitoring/IntegrationHealthCheck.{ts|java} — Per-integration health and metrics"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each ACL is a self-contained module with its own configuration, retry logic, and health checks. Domain aggregates use event sourcing or at minimum publish events via transactional outbox. All external calls are wrapped in circuit breakers. Every handler is idempotent. Use correlation IDs (generated at entry points: API, SFTP poll, webhook) that flow through all events for distributed tracing.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT let SAP XML structures leak into domain events — the ACL must fully translate","Do NOT process DHL webhooks synchronously — always enqueue and return fast","Do NOT share a single Kafka topic for internal events AND external Analytics — these have different lifecycle and schema governance needs","Do NOT skip the transactional outbox — writing to DB and publishing events as two separate actions WILL lose events under failure","Do NOT use a shared 'Integration' service that handles all three systems — this creates a god class and couples unrelated failure modes","Do NOT assume webhook delivery order matches real-world event order — always handle out-of-order and duplicate deliveries"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["ACL pattern per integration is clearly the right choice given three radically different protocols","Event-driven backbone is the natural fit given the Analytics Kafka requirement","Async webhook processing is mandatory given the 500ms SLA","Transactional outbox is necessary for reliable event publishing"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the internal event bus should be Kafka (reuse Analytics infra) or a lighter-weight broker (NATS, RabbitMQ) — depends on team expertise and operational capacity","Exact retry/backoff parameters for SAP SFTP polling and DHL webhook reprocessing — these need tuning based on real failure patterns","Whether SAP sends incremental files or full snapshots — this affects the idempotency strategy in the SAP ACL"],"assumptions":["SAP pushes XML files to SFTP (platform polls); SAP does not pull from the platform","DHL webhook signatures can be validated synchronously within the 500ms budget","The Analytics Kafka topic is managed by the Analytics team; the logistics platform only produces to it","Internal events are the system of record — no other system has write authority over order/shipment state","The platform has infrastructure to run a message broker (Kafka or equivalent)"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Integration architecture for a logistics platform connecting three external systems (SAP ERP via SFTP/XML, DHL Carrier via REST/webhooks, Analytics via Kafka) using an event-driven backbone with dedicated Anti-Corruption Layers per integration. The design treats internal domain events as the single source of truth, with each external system adapter translating to/from the internal model independently.
